type_of_disease.quote: Ascochyta leaf blight is caused by nearly 80 different species of Ascochyta . This fungus has been documented on many turfgrasses

value: Foliar
affected_parts.quote: The pathogen only attacks the foliage; therefore, damage is seldom permanent and recovery is quick under favorable growing conditions.

summary.value: Large irregular patches of turf rapidly turn straw-colored and appear dead, with leaves exhibiting bleached tip dieback extending approximately a third to halfway down the leaf blade.
summary.quote: Large irregular patches of turf rapidly turn a straw-color and appear dead. ... Leaves infected with the Ascochyta fungus often exhibit a bleached tip dieback that extends approximately a third to halfway down the leaf blade.

diagnostic_features.value: Bleached tip dieback with an abrupt, slightly pinched margin between healthy and diseased tissue (lacking the dark brown to purple banding of dollar spot), and minute yellow to dark brown flask-shaped pycnidia peppered throughout the dead leaves.
diagnostic_features.quote: The margin between healthy and diseased tissue is abrupt and slightly pinched but doesn't have the dark brown to purple banding that is characteristic of another disease called dollar spot. ... The Ascochyta fungus produces minute yellow to dark brown, flask-shaped fungal fruiting bodies called pycnidia in diseased leaf tissue. These fruiting bodies, which are easier to view with the aid of a hand lens, are peppered throughout the dead leaves and can be very useful as a diagnostic feature.

value: drought stress
dollar spot
heat stress
look_alikes.quote: The overall appearance of the disease may resemble drought stress, except that the symptoms of Ascochyta blight appear quickly ... doesn't have the dark brown to purple banding that is characteristic of another disease called dollar spot. ... These leaf symptoms resemble heat or drought stress.

value: Irrigate in the early morning hours (deep and infrequent) only when needed to prevent drought stress.
Reduce thatch and promote water penetration through the soil by yearly aerification.
Avoid excessive applications of nitrogen fertilizer, especially in spring; use slow-release nitrogen fertilizers and keep N below 1 lb/M.
Maintain a balanced fertilization program.
Maintain grass height at recommended levels (between 2.5 and 3 inches for Kentucky bluegrass).
Mow on a regular schedule with sharp mower blades when the canopy is dry, and collect clippings when the disease is active.
Avoid mowing during wet weather, and reduce mowing frequency / increase mowing height during outbreaks.
Maintain uniform soil moisture and verify irrigation uniformity to avoid drought stress.
Fungicides are not typically warranted; affected turf usually recovers after conducive conditions pass. If used, QoI and DMI fungicides are likely effective.
treatments.quote: Reduce thatch and promote water penetration through the soil by yearly aerification. Maintain grass height between 2 1/2 and 3 inches. Minimize wounding of the leaf blades by maintaining sharp mower blades. Avoid mowing during wet weather, especially when Ascochyta blight is active. Reduce mowing frequency and increase mowing height during Ascochyta outbreaks. ... Maintain a balanced fertilization program. ... Avoid excessive applications of nitrogen fertilizer, especially in spring. ... Try to maintain uniform soil moisture. ... Although several fungicides will inhibit Ascochyta growth, they can be expensive and difficult to apply.

conflicts.note: Only Bugwoodwiki provides a scientific name field (genus 'Ascochyta'). Other sources refer to 'Ascochyta spp.' in prose. No NCBI/MycoBank taxonomy validation was supplied; the genus is given because nearly 80 Ascochyta species have been documented on turfgrass (per NC State Extension).

conflicts.canonical_value: Bleached tip dieback with an abrupt, slightly pinched margin between healthy and diseased tissue (lacking the dark brown to purple banding of dollar spot), and minute yellow to dark brown flask-shaped pycnidia peppered throughout the dead leaves.
conflicts.alternative_value: Pycnidia appear as small brown or black freckles on infected leaves; blighting girdles the leaf from the tip down, producing straw-colored irregular patches.
conflicts.note: Sources describe pycnidia color differently: CSU says 'yellow to dark brown, flask-shaped'; MU says 'small brown or black freckles'; NC State says 'brownish, red, or black'; Bugwoodwiki says 'yellow brown, rust brown, or brick red, 70-200 micrometers'. These are consistent (variable pigmentation in pycnidia) rather than contradictory.

value: Foliar
Stem
Root
affected_parts.quote: The turfgrass in these patches is often completely dead, with infested roots, stolons, and rhizomes appearing black, brittle and severely rotted.

summary.value: The disease appears as randomly distributed, straw-colored, mostly circular patches ranging from a few inches to several feet in diameter, with completely dead turfgrass and black, brittle, rotted roots, stolons, and rhizomes.
summary.quote: The disease appears as randomly distributed, straw-colored, mostly circular patches ranging from a few inches to several feet in diameter. The turfgrass in these patches is often completely dead, with infested roots, stolons, and rhizomes appearing black, brittle and severely rotted.

diagnostic_features.value: Black brittle roots, rhizomes, and stolons surrounded by a dark network of hyphae; symptoms first evident in spring as uninfected turfgrass breaks dormancy and greens up.
diagnostic_features.quote: Spring dead spot is most often diagnosed based on symptom timing combined with the observation of black brittle roots, rhizomes, and stolons surrounded by a dark network of hyphae.

value: necrotic ring spot
look_alikes.quote: Ophiosphaerella spp. also cause disease on alternative hosts, such as necrotic ring spot caused by O. korrae on Kentucky bluegrass and fescues.

value: proper nitrogen fertility
regular cultivation practices and soil pH modification
Increasing mowing height prior to winter dormancy
use of acidifying fertilizer sources, such as ammonium sulfate
calcium nitrate applications (for O. korrae)
routine hollow-tine aerification and verticutting
use of bermudagrass cultivars with improved cold tolerance (e.g., 'Midfield', 'Midlawn', 'Tifsport')
treatments.quote: Management of the disease with cultural practices centers on proper nitrogen fertility, regular cultivation practices and soil pH modification... Increasing mowing height prior to winter dormancy may reduce spring dead spot severity in some vegetative cultivars... The use of acidifying fertilizer sources, such as ammonium sulfate, has reduced spring dead spot incidence caused by O. herpotricha. Conversely, spring dead spot caused by O. korrae may be reduced with calcium nitrate applications... Cultivation practices, such as routine hollow-tine aerification and verticutting, that improve drainage and cause rhizome and stolon regrowth have also been demonstrated to reduce spring dead spot incidence... Some of the cold tolerant cultivars that have demonstrated partial spring dead spot resistance include 'Midfield', 'Midlawn', and 'Tifsport'
